data.title: Kraft Heinz Vs. Mondelez: Same Roots, Diverging Trajectories
data.description:
Intro

Having done quite a few food‑stock analyses lately in search of potentially undeservedly beaten‑down names, I couldn’t leave The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) untouched. Once considered a symbol of a rock‑solid defensive name, it has struggled for almost a decade up to this day.

Looking further into the Kraft Heinz story, I discovered it was actually spun off from its parent company, Kraft Foods, in 2012. Following the split, Kraft Foods was renamed Mondelez International, Inc. (MDLZ), the well-known global leader in confectionery and snacks.

When I compared the two stock charts, the difference was striking. Let’s just say the chocolate and snacks business has performed far better than the remaining packaged‑grocery segment.

Shareholders of Kraft’s former parent company who held onto Mondelez have been rewarded very well, while the KHC shares (formerly Kraft Foods Group before its merger with Heinz) that they received as dividends have strongly underperformed.

Shortly, over the last 15 years, Mondelez has delivered a 177% return to its shareholders, while Kraft Heinz has reduced investors' capital by roughly 18%, even after accounting for dividends.

When I look at that chart, I can’t help wondering whether now might be the moment when, say, over the next 15 years, KHC could actually outperform Mondelez. At first glance, it doesn’t seem utopian at all. KHC appears to have adopted a strong “whatever it takes” approach, aligning all its efforts to adapt to the changing marketplace and get the business back into better shape, with a new CEO at the helm as well. At the same time, MDLZ operates in a category that is widely acknowledged as being among the most challenged currently, as consumers shift toward healthier and lower‑carb options.

That said, Mondelez's stock has been so resilient that the market now values it at $74 billion versus KHC’s $27 billion. For context, the two generate revenues of $38.5 billion and $25 billion, respectively. It means the market values Mondelez's one revenue dollar roughly two times more than Kraft's.

It’s fair to say the market hasn’t even started factoring in Mondelez’s need to strongly adapt to marketplace changes. A brief look at both companies’ P/E ratios, based on this year's estimates, shows a multiple of 11 for KHC and 19 for Mondelez. It almost feels like the snacking and confectionery giant is operating on a different planet, untouched by the food industry selloff happening on Earth.

These were my initial thoughts when I decided to compare the two head-to-head to better understand why the market values them so differently and to see which might offer better returns for investors over the next 15 years.

Kraft Heinz In Brief

History

While Mondelez and Kraft Heinz share the same early roots—going back to the founding of J.L. Kraft & Bros. Company in 1909 by James L. Kraft—Kraft's modern history effectively begins in 2012, when the businesses were split. The new company became Kraft Foods Group and was spun off, while the original global snacks division was renamed Mondelez International.

In 2015, Kraft merged with Heinz, a company founded by Henry J. Heinz, who started his first business in the 1870s.

Today, Kraft Heinz has approximately 35,000 employees globally based in 40 countries. It has two headquarters, one in Chicago and one in Pennsylvania. The first was the original headquarters of Kraft Foods, and the second is where Heinz has traditionally had its main office.

The company is among the world’s top 10 largest food producers and 3rd in the U.S. after Tyson Foods (TSN) and Mondelez.

Latest Results

The latest FY25 earnings report showed declines across all metrics, except for free cash flow.

Looking further behind the numbers, North America was the weakest performer, while developed markets actually grew.

The latest quarter beat expectations on the bottom line but slightly missed on the top line.

Outlook

The revised 2026 profit forecast of $1.98 to $2.10 per share fell short of the Street’s $2.49 estimate.

Despite mixed earnings, a weak outlook, and the unexpected decision to cancel the company’s separation plan, the stock managed to stay fairly steady for weeks around the $25 support level. But after drifting sideways for a while following the news, it eventually took a sharp drop and is now down about 11% as of this writing.

Mondelez In Brief

As mentioned in the KHC brief, the companies’ shared journey traces its roots back to 1909.

Today, Mondelez employs 91,000 people and is headquartered in Chicago. Here’s how they present their business:

Mondelēz International empowers people to snack right in over 150 countries around the world. We're leading the future of snacking with iconic global and local brands such as Oreo, Ritz, LU, Clif Bar and Tate's Bake Shop biscuits and baked snacks, as well as Cadbury Dairy Milk, Milka and Toblerone chocolate.

We are one of the largest snack companies in the world with global net revenues of approximately $36 billion in 2024. We hold the #1 global position in biscuits (cookies and crackers) and #2 in chocolate, while we’re growing rapidly in baked snacks. We also make and sell gum & candy as well as various cheese & grocery and powdered beverage products in certain markets.

Latest Earnings

The earnings were strong, but as shown in the presentation, they were driven by significant price increases that offset volume declines, which actually accelerated in Q4 to 4.8% from the year’s average of 3.7%.

Chocolate was the top performer with 11.4% growth but at the same time saw a significant 9.4% drop in volumes in Q4, accelerating from the annualized 7.5% decline. From a volume perspective, the best performer was Gum & Candy, which fell by just 0.7% year-over-year in Q4.

The quarterly results beat expectations on both the top and bottom lines:

Outlook

MDLZ is targeting 3-5% long-term top-line growth, but 2026 projections point to weaker performance—ranging from flat to slightly positive—while free cash flow is expected to remain steady at 2025 levels.

The stock has traded mostly sideways since the earnings release, dipping about 3% from the release date to today:

Head-To-Head Comparison

Kraft Heinz Mondelez
Key risks The portfolio is not keeping pace with customers’ changing habits, and sales are declining consistently The categories they operate in are among the most negatively affected by shifting consumer eating habits
Opportunities With a new CEO in place, the company is accelerating efforts to regain profitable growth Further leverage strong pricing power and adjust the portfolio to better align with market trends. Chocolate input prices are declining, creating potential profitability upside
Cyclicality dependence low low to medium
Current FY Revenue Growth decline 1.5% to 3.5% growth 0 to 2%
2027-2028 annualized revenue growth flat to 1% 3%
Current FY EPS Growth decline 22% growth 3.7%
2027-2028 EPS growth 5% Average growth 9.3%
EBITDA margin 2025 23% 13%
EV/EBITDA FWD 8.9 13.9
Free Cash Flow TTM/Dividend 193% 130%
Net Debt/EBITDA 3 3.82
Projected share repurchase No exact figure has been provided, but they have maintained their commitment to shareholder returns. In 2025, buybacks totaled $436 million. Guided $2 billion
Dividend safety high medium to high

Summary & Conclusions

To sum up, in my view, both companies have quite a challenging road ahead when it comes to adapting their portfolios. KHC still needs to show it can deliver at least flattish top-line performance, while MDLZ must find a way to slow the volume decline, which cannot be offset by price increases indefinitely.

Mondelez probably has some pricing power left, and the decline in cocoa prices provides another opportunity for profitability leverage. KHC shareholders will have to place their hopes on the new CEO to stop the sales decline.

MDLZ, meanwhile, has somewhat higher exposure to commodity cyclicality, as cocoa and sugar are key inputs. That said, their pricing power offsets this reasonably well, as previously seen.

Mondelez is showing stronger growth metrics, while KHC is likely to see only minimal bottom-line improvement in the coming years.

KHC’s core profitability profile is stronger, with an EBITDA margin as high as 22% versus Mondelez at 13%.

KHC also trades at a more attractive EV/EBITDA multiple, though MDLZ arguably deserves a higher multiple given its growth outlook.

In terms of dividend safety, KHC is the clear winner. Mondelez’s leverage ratio has increased, and the company will need to manage its finances very carefully to maintain investment‑grade status while supporting both the dividend and substantial buybacks.

I rate both companies as Hold, but my pick is KHC. It’s trading at low multiples and offers a solid, well-covered 7% dividend. Moreover, if its sales decline stabilizes over the next few quarters, I will seriously consider an upgrade.

Mondelez, with a P/E of 19 and EV/EBITDA of 13.9, looks fully priced in my view. Given the category it operates in is among the most challenged considering GLP-1 headwinds, the HOLD is more of a neutral stance, as Mondelez shareholders seem as resilient to higher prices as its confectionery and snack buyers.

data.title: Smart Hedge Trades For A Fragile Ceasefire
data.description:
The Iran ceasefire is technically still in place, but barely. The first round of peace talks ended without agreement, the White House has threatened a naval blockade of Iranian shipping (and tolled) traffic, and President Trump said Iranian ships impeding US warships would be eliminated. The ceasefire has not collapsed, but it is hard to find a silver lining as things currently stand.

We started writing this piece last week, positioning for a low-probability breakdown hedge that the market was ignoring. The probability is no longer low. The ceasefire is fragile enough that a single headline can swing oil six percent in either direction, and the market is struggling to price a wide distribution of outcomes. We decided to put together a menu of hedge trades, five longs and five shorts, for a potential ceasefire breakdown.

Traditional wisdom during previous Middle East flare-ups has been to go long defense, and short airlines. In our view, there is limited alpha in these trades. We are looking for derivative positions that the market has not fully priced-in.

Where Things Stand

The ceasefire, which took effect on April 8th, is still nominally holding, but the diplomatic route has been erratic. US-Iran talks over the weekend ended without agreement, and both sides are back in their respective corners. The White House has openly threatened a Hormuz blockade while minesweeping activities were conducted, and the US military has said it can blockade Iranian ports if ordered. When the market first digested the threat, WTI spiked above $100. It has since pulled back to the high $80s on the White House floating renewed peace talks. The structural case does not change on a single headline.

Despite this, the ceasefire terms themselves were always narrow. They address direct state-on-state strikes and leave gaps for proxy activity in Iraq, Syria, Yemen, and Lebanon. There is nothing enforceable on Iran’s nuclear timeline. Stars and Stripes ran a headline yesterday characterizing the ceasefire as “hang[ing] by a thread” while the blockade took effect. Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu told reporters that the ceasefire "could end at any moment" and that Israel backs the blockade. CBS reported that the US Navy has begun its blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz. Those are not the headlines of a stable ceasefire.

Crude came full circle, from $104 during the opening week of the war back to the $80s on ceasefire hopes, shot back above $100 on blockade headlines, and now sits near $88 on new peace talks. The market is oscillating between two scenarios: a long-term ceasefire and renewed conflict. Our base case assumes the status quo sustains long enough for the hedges to pay out, even if the ceasefire ultimately holds.

Longs: Tankers, LNG, Fertilizer, and Drones

International Seaways (INSW)

Frontline (FRO) is the largest listed crude tanker operator. This company has the most press coverage, and it is the ticker that retail traders often pair with Brent momentum. At around $35 with a ~21x P/E, it is also too crowded and leaves little room at current valuations.

International Seaways is the better version of the same trade. At around $75, the stock trades at a roughly 12x trailing multiple, nearly a 40% discount to Frontline on earnings. Market cap is ~$3.7 billion against Frontline’s ~$7.9 billion, which means less sell-side coverage and passive-fund ownership. The fleet mix is what matters if Hormuz disruption moves from threat to reality: a blended inventory of VLCCs, Suezmaxes, and LR1s. When the strait is facing blockades, you do not just want crude tankers. You want the full logistics stack, and INSW is a one-stop shop.

INSW trades near its 52-week high of $78.51, so it’s not completely overlooked. But at a 12x multiple, there is enough meat on the bone to make INSW a sufficient tail-risk hedge. If the ceasefire stabilizes and tensions ease, the stock probably drifts back toward $65. If the ceasefire fails and disruption increases, $100 is easily achievable.

Scorpio Tankers (STNG)

Scorpio Tankers is the world’s largest owner/operator of product tankers, focused exclusively on transporting refined petroleum products like gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel rather than unrefined crude. STNG is the cheapest of the major listed tanker stocks on a P/E basis. The current price is ~$73, the trailing P/E ~11x, market cap is ~$3.96 billion, and the dividend yield is 2.35%.

With Hormuz disruption, the arbitrage that matters is not just crude oil, but refined product flows from Gulf refineries to Europe and Asia. This routing lengthens voyages and compresses global tanker supply. The rate spike for product tankers in a Hormuz scenario is typically larger in percentage terms than the crude tanker spike, because the fleets are smaller and more geographically concentrated.

STNG has cut leverage drastically in recent years. Their balance sheet is the strongest it’s been in a decade. For a hedge trade, STNG offers the best blend of valuation, balance sheet strength, and upside among the tanker group. Pairing it with INSW gives full petroleum-refined product coverage.

Flex LNG (FLNG)

Our LNG pick is still not on any Iran-war trade list we have seen. Flex LNG trades at around $30, with a market cap of nearly $1.6 billion, and delivers a 10% dividend yield.

Qatar is the world’s second-largest LNG exporter and ships its entire output through the Persian Gulf. Hormuz disruption does not just affect crude. It disrupts LNG flows to Europe and Asia at a moment when European inventories are lean, and the EU’s pivot away from Russian gas has made Gulf LNG increasingly important. Flex LNG operates a fleet of MEGI and XDF carriers on long-term charters. The company delivers a strong dividend, and investors should not be worried about short-term yield changes. With its existing long-term contracts, the company is locked into favorable terms, allowing investors to earn yield while the geopolitical wave passes.

If the ceasefire holds and tensions calm, the 10% yield pays you to wait on a conservatively financed shipping stock. If the ceasefire fails and Gulf LNG flows are disrupted, charter rates spike, and FLNG sees further upside on charter renewals. Flex is a trade to consider for investors looking for sector trades that deliver yield, despite intraday swings.

CF Industries (CF)

CF Industries is a nitrogen-based fertilizer producer, trading around $117 with a ~13x P/E and a ~$18 billion market cap. Fertilizer stocks are often overlooked in this conflict. In our view, two things are worth considering. First, natural gas is the primary input expense for nitrogen fertilizer, and Gulf LNG disruption has pushed prices up. That hits EU and Asian producers harder than it hits CF, which sources from cheap North American gas. CF’s margin spread over the global cost curve widens. Second, a Middle East oil shock historically drives inflation across commodities, and fertilizer prices tend to move with grain prices.

CF has traded between $70 and $140 for two years as revenue stagnated and the urea market remained oversupplied. At 13.6x P/E, the stock is priced moderately. An oil shock breaks the range. Even without one, you are paying a fair multiple for a well-capitalized, gas-advantaged producer with embedded tail-risk optionality. With WTI near $88, that protection is starting to look more real.

Kratos Defense (KTOS)

We are not recommending Lockheed or Northrop because too many people are already in those trades. Instead, Kratos Defense is our contrarian pick. The stock is down 45% from its 52-week high of $134.00 and trades at ~$72, with a market cap of nearly $14 billion.

Kratos makes drones, tactical unmanned aerial systems, loitering munitions, and other low-cost precision systems. These are the products that would be needed if the ceasefire fully breaks down. Enforcing a blockade in Hormuz is not an F-35-type mission. It is swarms of surveillance drones, counter-UAS defense, and loitering munitions to protect shipping lanes. That is what Kratos specializes in, not Lockheed.

Kratos’s P/E is distorted by heavy investment in the XQ-58A Valkyrie program and depressed trailing earnings. The stock sold off on the original ceasefire news, but in our view, that works to our benefit. We believe the dip creates an entry that looks better by the day. If things stabilize, KTOS likely grinds toward $80 on existing momentum. If the ceasefire fails, the stock likely moves toward the old highs on fundamentals and short covering. The stock is up from $70 last week, but still has ~$60 of upside between current prices and the 52-week high.

Shorts: Airlines, Cruise, Luxury, Homebuilders, Trucking

Delta Air Lines (DAL)

Delta is the cleanest airline short in any oil shock because it does not hedge against jet fuel spikes the way most peers do, which makes its earnings more sensitive to crude prices. Its Monroe Energy refinery "natural hedge" does not fully offset crude spikes, so fuel cost moves hit earnings faster than at peers. Currently trading at ~$70, representing a ~9.8x P/E and a ~$44 billion market cap. In our view, shorting a weaker airline brings additional risk layers that muddy the waters.

DAL is up from $67.82 last week and jumped today on renewed-talks headlines. In our view, that makes the entry point better, not worse. Airlines priced a Hormuz reopening before it happened. If WTI spikes back above $95, DAL likely gives most of these gains back. Long-dated puts still offer better leverage than outright shorts and help traders avoid short-term squeezes on diplomatic machinations.

Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH)

The cruise short in an oil shock is Norwegian Cruise Line, trading at ~$21, with a ~23x P/E and a market cap of ~$9.2 billion. Fuel is always at the top of cruise line expenditures, and Norwegian is already highly leveraged. As demand pulls back in the current economic climate, the balance sheet has not recovered enough to absorb a concurrent oil shock.

Norwegian’s balance sheet is the worst among cruise majors. Long-term debt remains high compared to pre-COVID levels, and free cash flow is not enough to delever without revenue growth. The war, subsequent oil spikes, and weaker consumer demand across the Eastern Mediterranean are bad signs for Norwegian.

Norwegian is close to its 52-week low of $15.31, so some risk is priced in. But with oil prices rising and the ceasefire weakening, the next drop could happen faster than the market expects.

LVMH ADR (LVMUY)

LVMH, trading back at ~$113, is the hardest call on this list, and we want to be upfront about that. The stock is now just 7% above its 52-week low of $101.80, and far below its high of $152.95. The slowdown in China’s luxury market had already hurt the stock, and now the worsening ceasefire is making things more difficult.

Luxury is the cleanest second-derivative play on an EU consumer slump. Sustained Gulf oil shortages would renew EU inflation fears and compound the slowdown in China’s economy. If the ceasefire stabilizes, LVMH probably finds a floor around current prices. If it fails and oil stays above $90, the stock is likely heading below $100 as input costs rise and consumer demand deteriorates. Despite this, in our view, LVMH is not a clean short at these levels. It is a position that gets worse in a tail scenario, which is exactly the kind of exposure to fade in a hedge book.

DR Horton (DHI)

Here is another short that is not getting much attention. DR Horton is at ~$145, with a ~13 P/E and a nearly $42 billion market cap. The stock is down from its 52-week high but is still trading at high multiples compared to peer homebuilders. Its price depends on the market’s belief that the Fed will cut rates, a thesis unlikely to play out if the oil shock continues.

Higher oil prices drive inflation, widen breakevens, and decrease the likelihood of a Fed rate cut. DHI is the largest and most liquid company in its peer group and has the tightest gross margin sensitivity because of its dependence on mortgage buydowns to clear inventory.

With WTI back near $88 and Hormuz rhetoric escalating, the rate-cut narrative is under significant pressure. Homebuilders are not top of mind in geopolitics, which is the whole reason we like the setup. If the ceasefire holds and inflation keeps cooling, DHI is fine. If it fails, DHI has direct exposure to the monetary policy consequences of an oil-driven inflation shock.

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL)

Old Dominion Freight Line is at ~$209, with a ~$44 billion market cap and a ~43 P/E. It is trading within 5% of its 52-week high of $221.63. In our view, this is the most important short on the list.

ODFL is the most efficient LTL carrier in North America. Despite the operational integrity, the base case is simple: at nearly 43x earnings, ODFL is priced for a soft landing, and the cost structure is directly exposed to diesel prices at a moment when oil swings wildly each session. The multiple compresses when rates move against you, and the operating margin compresses when diesel rises before contract pricing can reset.

For a ceasefire-failure hedge, a premium-multiple stock trading near all-time highs with direct commodity cost exposure is the kind of short that delivers outsized returns. If the ceasefire stabilizes, ODFL grinds higher and the hedge costs little. If the ceasefire fails, ODFL is an asymmetric trade. We do not say that lightly, but valuation and exposure are what they are.

Risks

First, there could be a diplomatic breakthrough. The first round of talks has broken down, but they could start again. If the White House backs away from talk of a blockade and a new agreement covers proxy activity and nuclear inspections, most of these trades would reverse. Tanker and LNG stocks would lose their gains, the defense pick would go sideways, and the shorts would lose value as risk premiums fade. We think this is less likely now than last week, but it is still possible. Position sizes should reflect that.

Second, Saudi Arabia and the UAE could decide to fully make up for Iranian supply with their own production. OPEC+ has plenty of spare capacity, and if they act together, it would limit price volatility, though logistics would be the one wrench in the gears. For an oil shock to linger, there needs to be a supply disruption and no way to replace it. LNG and product tankers are less affected by this risk than crude tankers, which is why FLNG and STNG are our top long picks.

What To Watch

We find a helpful resource is reviewing crude tanker charter rates on the Ras Tanura to Ningbo route. These are updated weekly and usually move before stock prices when the market senses disruption risk. Another is the skew on long-dated Brent call options. If implied volatility on out-of-the-money Brent calls stays high, even as spot prices fall, the derivatives market is signaling long-term disruption risk.

The market is swinging violently between complacency and panic on every diplomatic development. This type of volatility can yield great returns for discerning investors, while everyone else chases the last headline.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3i Group: 18.5x Could Be Too High A Multiple For Action
data.description:
3i Group plc (TGOPF) is not our favorite. The majority of its NAV is dictated by Action, whose valuation is too high in our view relative to other European peers that are growing profit at similar rates. Moreover, we generally question whether it's wise to invest in PE-related stocks in the current environment, where the fading issue of high costs of capital that was permitting a return to market of PEs and was the main positive thing that 3i had in its favor as of our previous coverage is now back in force due to the reinflation effects of the Iran War. The declines in 3i's stock price have been justified particularly on tactical considerations around PE valuations and upward cost of capital pressure, and looking forward, we think the Action multiples are twice what they should be, and therefore, according to our assumptions, there would be no NAV discount and related margin of safety.

Valuation

Here are the top 10 investments by value for 3i, which uses a permanent prop capital model - they're not a typical GP whose business model depends on AUM, management fees, and carried interest, as they've raised money at the company level and use that to generate a return on investment. The total investment portfolio, including investments beyond the top 10, amounts to 30 billion GBP, and therefore a NAV of around 3,000 GBX, which is the way to assess the value of 3i at today's prices. The current share price is around 2,650 GBX. However, note the assumptions for the valuation of Action, the discount retailer and crown jewel of the 3i portfolio.

The multiple of 18.5x, net of the liquidity discount, remained unchanged, resulting in a valuation of £22,382 million for 3i’s 62.3% equity stake (30 September 2025: £21,464 million for a 60.1% stake).

Q3 performance update

These assumptions offer investors no margin of safety in our view. Consider other retailers, not even necessarily discount retailers carrying the sort of inventory Action carries, but things like clothes and groceries. Inditex (ISIN: ES0148396007) and owners of Zara are around 13x EV/EBITDA on a forward basis. They've had EBITDA growth of around 5% and are a very solid brand. Marks & Spencer (MAKSY) is at around 7x EV/EBITDA on a forward basis. They had 12% EBITDA growth. Europris ASA (ERPSY) is at 7x EV/EBITDA on a forward basis, and they have quite a similar type of inventory to Action. Note EBITDA growth of 7.5% for Europris. Action's EBITDA was up 14%, comparable to some of these picks (though a bit better than them), but with more than double the multiple at 18x, and that is after a liquidity discount. The stocks we are comparing Action to are already liquid. While it is true that Action is contemplating a US launch, it is competitive out there, and in principle these other stocks might also have markets that they still haven't tapped yet. Marks & Spencer is working on making their US strategy successful; they are struggling, but theoretically it's a new market for them as well. Their current growth is very comparable to Action. Ultimately, these are all retail stocks as well; the value-add in retail is not that great by our standards, where we prefer looking at things further up the value chain in general.

Action is more than 2/3 of the total 3i portfolio, so we do not judge 3i based on the provided NAV figure since we would not use the multiple they used for Action. We think a fairer NAV for 3i, assuming we'd have no problem with the valuations of the rest of the 3i portfolio, would be something below 2,000 GBX, which means 3i is trading at a premium, where there are plenty of discounted holdings out there with assets that we prefer.

Bottom Line

In circumstances where PE markets were seeing very frequent transactions, and considering the dry powder in the asset class, we may even be alright with a premium multiple applied in private markets, as 3i has done here. It hasn't been unheard of in the past that private market multiples were at premiums to public comps even though liquidity considerations would dictate the opposite should be the case. In our last coverage, we pointed out that there was some resumption in PE activity and M&A in general thanks to the rate cuts that finally came in 2025 in the US with inflation waning well below the 3% upper band, and that European PE had started to recover as well.

However, CPI readings are now at 3.3% in the US. Consumer sentiment is bad as well in the US because the Iran War has driven up prices at the pump, meaning inflation is front of mind for consumers and therefore likely baked into expectations. 3i is Europe-focused, but also Euro Area inflation is creeping up fast, and it has meaningfully impacted the yield curve, with 5-year rates up 25 bps and shorter-term rates up 50 bps since the beginning of the year. In fact, the latest data on global deal volumes from Axios shows a considerable toll taken by the Iran War on deal volumes already. With the blockade in place after failed ceasefire talks, the root of the energy crisis remains, and deal volumes may remain depressed, likely also in sponsor activity. We do not think that the PE markets are liquid enough yet to justify optimistic multiples, the likes of which we'd seen in 2021, which the industry thinks history will remember as a poor vintage.

We think there are plenty of better NAV plays out there.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Bargain EBITDA Multiples As GLP-1 Drama Passes
data.description:
In a surprisingly short amount of time, the S&P 500 has quickly shaken off the risk-off mentality and market jitters that have dominated since the conflict in Iran began. That said, many pockets of the stock market remain in deeply negative territory, particularly small- and mid-cap growth stocks, which have re-rated substantially downward.

Hims & Hers (HIMS), the personalized medications company, has seen one of the biggest re-ratings in the stock market to date. Shares are down nearly 40% since January, and relative to peaks above $60 briefly notched last year, the stock has lost two-thirds of its value. The question now is, when and how does Hims exit the bear market?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Hims in January, when the stock was trading at $33 per share. Needless to say, my bullishness was ill-timed, and I wasn't able to predict the sharp re-rating in small- and mid-cap growth that has taken place over the past few months. But when I take a look at this stock with fresh eyes, I see many appealing characteristics that make its freshly reduced valuation multiples a very compelling buy. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

With GLP-1 volatility in the rearview mirror, Hims & Hers' valuation is overdue for upward re-rating

As a reminder, over the past year, much of the volatility in Hims & Hers stock has concentrated around the unsure path to GLP-1 sales. Novo Nordisk, one of the principal makers of GLP-1 weight loss medications, initially sued Hims for infringing on its patent and selling an unauthorized compounded version of the drug after Novo exited its supply shortage status. In March, Novo Nordisk dropped its lawsuit against Hims & Hers. And then, beginning on May 26, Hims & Hers jointly announced with Novo Nordisk that it would be officially selling Wegovy and Ozempic on its platform (beginning at $149/month), effectively turning Hims & Hers into an authorized distribution channel for Novo Nordisk.

Meanwhile, an additional value-add revenue opportunity for Hims & Hers is a membership plan that is available as an add-on to weight loss medication. Priced at $149/month (on top of the regular cost of weight loss medication), this program gives patients access to ongoing medical check-ins, personalized nutrition plans that are meant to be used in conjunction with medication, and 24/7 support.

To me, the biggest upside opportunity for Hims & Hers is leaving the weight loss controversy behind in the rearview mirror. The company doesn't disclose exact revenue from weight loss, but notes that the majority of its revenue is outside this lower-margin specialty. With a favorable resolution for Hims & Hers with Novo Nordisk dropping its suit and agreeing to sell on the Hims & Hers platform, investors can now focus on the company's other growth catalysts: international expansion, growth of the Hers brand (which has now hit >$1 billion in ARR), and growth of new specialties. The company launched testosterone supplements in September 2025, perimenopause and menopause treatments in October, and diagnostic testing services in November.

With the distraction of GLP-1 behind us, Hims & Hers has a significant opportunity to re-rate its stock upward. At current share prices near $21, the stock trades at a market cap of $4.84 billion. Netting off the $928.8 million of cash against $972.6 million of convertible debt and $47.0 million of earn-outs due to M&A targets leaves us an enterprise value of $4.93 billion.

For FY26, Hims & Hers has guided to 15%-24% y/y growth in revenue to $2.7-$2.9 billion (we note the company should have higher visibility into its demand now than in FY25, when its continued sale of weight loss medication was still in question).

Where investors tripped up and penalized Hims was on its expectations for $300-$375 million in adjusted EBITDA, which is a wide 11%-13% range that represents downside from 13.5% margins in FY25.

Still, we note that we're compensated for risk in the form of a modest valuation at 14.6x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. The company is incurring near-term margin headwinds to expand into new overseas markets, which still represent a small contribution to revenue today. We have confidence in Hims & Hers' ability to scale profitably in new markets and new specialties, plus drive down corporate opex as a percentage of revenue. For a company that is still expecting to grow its top line at >20% y/y, Hims still represents "growth at a reasonable price."

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Hims' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Hims & Hers' revenue grew 28% y/y to $617.8 million, slightly beating Wall Street's expectations of $617.3 million in the quarter. Meanwhile, we note that the company has continued to deepen its bench of active subscribers. It ended FY26 with 2.5 million active subscribers, adding 282k net-new subscribers on a year/y basis. Meanwhile, full-year revenue grew 59% y/y, with international revenue growing ~5x y/y to $134 million (still a small 6% contribution to the overall top line).

Again, we have to emphasize here that Hims is making a deep marketing push into brand-new territory, primarily in Canada and large European markets (UK, Ireland, Germany, France, and Spain). This is what is causing margin drag in the near term in FY26, but it's a growth opportunity that substantially increases the company's TAM. Management believes it has an addressable population of 200+ million potential subscribers in Europe, versus 280 million in the U.S. and Canada.

Meanwhile, we note that the company recently announced its $1.15 billion acquisition of Eucalyptus (on a deferred payment schedule, with $240 million of consideration paid upfront and the rest through earn-outs), which is a digital healthcare platform with a large presence in Australia and Japan, which are brand-new markets for Hims & Hers. The deal is expected to close in mid-2026, and isn't included in the company's guidance targets.

Investors are nervous that international markets may take years to scale to profitability, and that the company isn't expecting Eucalyptus to be meaningfully accretive to profits in the near term. Per CEO Andrew Dudum's remarks on the Q4 earnings call explaining the rationale behind the deal:

Eucalyptus deploys a rigorous capital allocation framework similar to our own. They currently have an annual revenue run rate north of $450 million and strong execution enabled them to drive triple-digit year-over-year growth in each quarter of 2025, while also maintaining line of sight to profitability. While we do not expect the business to drive meaningful adjusted EBITDA losses, we do not expect to drive meaningful margin expansion for several years in our international business.

Across the majority of international markets, we expect to take a growth-oriented approach and focus on reaching as many consumers as possible before focusing on the margin expansion efforts, even if that means running certain markets at or near breakeven on an adjusted EBITDA margin basis. We saw this approach work in the U.S. and will utilize a similar playbook to progressively expand markets towards margin expansion in the future. This is our largest acquisition to date at up to $1.15 billion of total consideration. The upfront payment at close is expected to be approximately $240 million, with the remaining payments for guaranteed consideration and earnouts to be made through 2029.

Still, what gives us comfort is clear evidence that in mature markets, Hims & Hers can drive down opex as its subscriber pool grows. As shown in the chart below, marketing expenses reduced 6 points as a percentage of revenue to 38% in the most recent quarter:

This helped to offset a 5-point reduction in gross margins to 72% (due to a heavier mix of lower-margin weight loss sales), which positioned adjusted EBITDA at $66.3 million, reflecting 28% y/y growth at a flat 11% margin.

We expect Hims & Hers to continue scaling adjusted EBITDA margins gradually as its international markets mature, and the potential for the company to slow headcount growth (and achieve M&A synergies) by leveraging AI and automation also reflects potential upside. New high-margin offerings, such as diagnostic lab testing (which gives customers data on 130+ biomarkers), will also help the company's profitability profile. The company expects lab testing to hit $100 million in ARR in "the near future", not counting the cross-sell opportunity into recommended supplements and medications that the testing may reveal.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are risks that we need to keep in mind as well. As a reminder, Hims & Hers' moat lies in its ownership of the customer relationship, and in its base of subscribers (two-thirds of whom are on personalized plans) - but the company owns no IP. It may see its gross margin profile (currently in the low 70%s) erode further if consumers buy directly from pharmaceutical brands or from new marketplaces like TrumpRx.

We note as well that Hims & Hers is a scale business, and it doesn't have the capital to buy out its competitors in every new market it enters (its balance sheet won't support multiple Eucalyptus-size acquisitions). It may fail to scale in its new markets, which may put pressure on adjusted EBITDA margins in the long haul.

That said, I still see more potential upsides than downsides in this stock, especially at a low/mid-teens adjusted EBITDA multiple that underappreciates the stock's high-20%s growth rate. Stay long here and use this year's dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: ONEOK: A Midstream C-Corp To Keep Adding
data.description:
ONEOK, Inc. (NYSE: OKE) is a more than $50 billion midstream company, with an around 5% dividend yield. The company is a C-corp with a traditional payout structure versus other MLP K-1 midstream companies. The company has heavily outperformed the market since we last recommended investing, with a cautious ratings upgrade, but we still recommend investing at this time.

ONEOK Investment Overview

ONEOK has an impressive ~60k mile pipeline network with major assets connected to all the major centers.

Most importantly, the company has "full-cycle" connections. That means that not only does it take natural gas liquids and gas to the major refining and distribution centers in Oklahoma and Texas, but it takes refined products back to major population centers like Chicago. This is a resilient fee-based model where Oneok is well connected.

ONEOK's portfolio is well distributed across these different business segments. The company's 2026 guidance for EBITDA is $8.1 billion, well distributed across its geographic and commodity cycles. It's important here that the company's business has a minimal crude oil demand component. In our view, crude oil has more climate change, etc., risk than natural gas, especially with LNG growth.

We expect ONEOK's natural gas portfolio to position the company incredibly well for long-term cash flow. Additionally, refined products and crude pipelines tend to move into population centers. That means not only do they have stable consumer demand, but population centers are very hard places to build pipelines, meaning a tough-to-replace asset.

ONEOK 2026 Guidance

ONEOK's 2026 guidance does have some concerns; however, we're optimistic in some regards.

First, the company is betting on weak oil and gas prices, weaker than 2025 prices that took a hit after "liberation day policies". It expects ~0.2% EBITDA growth, but that's at prices of $55-60 WTI. The U.S.-Iran-Israel war has pushed prices much higher, and while ONEOK has strong fee-based contracts, it still benefits some from higher prices.

We do have some concern that ONEOK has traditionally had a double-digit EPS growth rate that's slowing down. Obviously the company is impacted by lower prices, but with 2026 guidance sitting at around $5.46/share in EPS, that's a big slowdown in growth. That's especially true given the company's P/E of just under 16x, which is a slightly higher valuation for the industry.

However, as seen above, it should benefit from higher prices, and we expect them to come in on the higher end here. The growth of natural gas and oil exports from the U.S., with U.S. oil exports hitting a record, given the Strait of Hormuz blockage, will only serve to help Oneok here, in our view.

Oneok has some exciting growth projects that we feel integrate well into its remaining asset portfolio. For example, we already discussed how its refined products to major population centers system was unique, and the company is able to do a bolt-on project to account for rising demand in the Denver area. Adding additional fractionation and processing capacity is also key.

These projects will mostly come out in 2027 through 2028, with some major projects for 2028. For example, the Eiger Express pipeline adds key takeaway capacity to a Permian Basin market sitting at negative natural gas prices. These projects meet Oneok's high capital allocation multiple requirements of 4-6x EBITDA.

The Texas City Logistics has ~$1 billion in ONEOK's share capital (investor presentation slide 15), which means ~$200 million in fresh EBITDA when it comes online in 2028, or several % EBITDA growth.

Capital Allocation

ONEOK's capital allocation plans continue to support lofty shareholder returns.

It returned $2.7 billion to shareholders in 2025, a 5% shareholder return yield. The company's dividend is almost 5%, along with some buybacks. ONEOK continues to opportunistically invest in high-return growth, and we'd like to see it ramp that up at the 4-6x historical EBITDA multiple. Oneok is targeting 3-4% annualized growth with its 5% dividend and ~85% payout.

The company has >25 years of dividend stability and strong historic growth. We expect the company to be able to maintain its 3-4% annual growth rate, which is something that investors should keep a close eye on. ONEOK's $2 billion share repurchase authorization is almost 4% of its outstanding shares, and with a 3.5x debt-to-EBITDA ratio, it can comfortably afford these returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is ONEOK's valuation. The company trades at a P/E of almost 16x, which is a decently high valuation (just over 6% earnings yield) versus long-term S&P 500 returns of ~10%. The company needs to achieve strong earnings growth to justify that valuation, and with weak guidance in 2026, the company's 2027-8 growth projects need to succeed.

Conclusion

ONEOK has an impressive portfolio of assets. It has long-term natural gas assets and refined product delivery assets that are hard to replicate in major population centers. The company has an almost 5% dividend yield it can comfortably afford, and it's guiding for 3-4% continued dividend growth. The company needs to sustain that to justify its valuation.

Oneok has a number of impressive growth opportunities in 2027-2028, for example, exciting bolt-ons to the Denver area. The company's 2026 guidance is weak; however, that's with guidance for weak prices. With the US-Iran-Israel war, prices are high, and while the company has mostly fee-based capital, we expect it to benefit, making it a valuable investment.

data.title: Saudi Arabia's Most Valuable Brand Takes The Spotlight: Aramco
data.description:
Saudi Arabia is the most vital hydrocarbon player in the Middle East. Its national jewel, Saudi Aramco (ARMCO), listed on the Saudi exchange Tadawul, is its most valuable brand, reaching a $6.6 trillion valuation. For reference, Nvidia (NVDA) is $4.6 trillion, and Aramco is 10 times that of Exxon Mobil (XOM). Even in writing this, the volatility and fluidity of the Iran crisis situation is changing daily. However, the resilience of the global oil and gas industry is unparalleled. Aramco is nearly 100 years old, and with peak oil demand yet to be seen or determined, the sands of time for oil are shifting again.

Most important in this crisis currently is the physical shortage. Then comes inflation. The futures market may say all kinds of things about longer-term expectations. However, global economies live in the physical world. Ask India, Pakistan, and many Southeast Asian countries.

Restoring Flows

Wood Mackenzie ("WoodMac"), in OilPrice.com, noted that,

“even if the Strait of Hormuz opened today without any restrictions and risks, oil and gas supply from the Middle East faces recovery of several months well into the late summer."

At present, it appears that the U.S. and the resilience of Saudi Arabia and the Gulf State producers, the OPEC core, will adapt. This isn’t their first rodeo.

This chart below shows some of the dynamics in trying to resume flows through the infrastructure surrounding Saudi Arabia.

After a reopening and the workarounds in place, the action shifts to producing the resources that have been shut in. Analysts at WoodMac note:

“Once shipping constraints smooth, the constraints on supply will shift to the upstream production, and this will expose the different challenges each country faces.”

They expect Iraq to resume levels that were pre-crisis in 6 to 9 months. Fortunately, Saudi Arabia and the UAE have space capacity. There has only been about 1-1.5 million barrels per day (m/b/d) of spare capacity surrounding the crisis timeframe, according to a Middle East expert from an energy conference I attended April 9. He noted that the spare capacity that OPEC normally manages has value too, like the U.S.’s ability to be a swing producer, a position largely held by Saudi Arabia in the past.

Reuters reported OPEC’s recent assessment:

Crude oil output by OPEC+ averaged 35.06 million bpd in March, down 7.70 million bpd from February, citing secondary sources, the report showed, with Iraq and Saudi Arabia making the biggest cuts.

OPEC+ agreed ⁠on ​April 5 to raise its oil output quotas by 206,000 bpd ​for May, a modest rise that will largely exist on paper as long as key members are unable to raise production ​due to the effective Hormuz blockage.”

Supply and Pricing Scenarios

Iraq and Saudi Arabia are the largest producers in OPEC central, i.e., excluding Russia. Most notably, views differ on 2026 demand, with OPEC saying world oil demand will rise by 1.38 million bpd in 2026, and the U.S. EIA expects half of that now.

OilPrice.com wrote on March 18th:

Commodity experts at Standard Chartered have estimated that 7.4-8.2 million barrels per day (mb/d) of supply is currently offline after the Hormuz blockade, with Saudi Arabia estimated to have lost 2.0-2.5 mb/d, 2.9 mb/d by Iraq, 0.5-0.8 mb/d by the UAE, 0.5mb/d by Qatar and 0.5 mb/d by Kuwait.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) laid out its case for the rest of the year. In March, production of 7.5 million barrels per day (b/d) of crude oil production was shut in. April is projected at 9.1 million b/d of shut-in production, assuming an end to the conflict in April and cargoes resume through the Strait of Hormuz. In that case, shut-ins will fall to 6.7 million b/d in May and return close to pre-conflict levels in late 2026. OPEC recently highlighted that production has declined by 7.56 million barrels per day to 22 million b/d in March owing to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

On the pricing front, the Dubai oil basket has been most relevant to Asian markets, as it is the pricing for the 80% of the market that originates from the Persian Gulf. The spot price reached $170 at one point, according to the Saudi analyst from the symposium. This shows the impact of the physical shortages, not necessarily the expectations built into the futures markets. Platts, which reports Dubai oil futures, has April quoted at $105 on April 14 and $94 for May.

The Dallas Federal Reserve economists attempt to develop scenarios of how the duration of the crisis increases prices. They find:

• An outage that only lasts one quarter would raise the average WTI price (CL1:COM) to $110 in April 2026; two quarters causes the WTI price to peak at $132 in July 2026; three quarters is now unlikely, with a $167 peak by October.

Aramco Stock Talk

Given the market tightness ahead, I would expect ARMCO to trade in an upper band until oil prices resettle in the next few months, barring any new shocks. Exposure to the stock can also be gained via the iShares MSCI Saudi Arabia ETF (KSA), the Saudi Arabia exchange-traded fund.

ARMCO, called “Saudi Arabian Oil Co.” as termed in KSA, is valued at $6.6 trillion as of April 14th; its P/E is around 19 with a 4.57% dividend.

Outlook: More Than Just Oil

If Saudi Arabia has any say in the region, which it does, they will want to restore stability and continue diversifying its economy according to the Vision 2030 plan. Since the onset of the national goal of economic diversification, the AI investment theme has been gaining momentum. Project Transcendence, a $100 billion government-backed initiative to develop an AI hub, is also threatened. Both Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL) have invested large sums, $80 billion and $14 billion, respectively, in a firm called HUMAIN, a Saudi-backed AI investment. Google Cloud (GOOG) has a $10 billion investment, and Qualcomm (QCOM) signed an MOU, both to support AI- and cloud-based infrastructure via HUMAIN.

Similarly to the U.S. push in the AI infrastructure buildout, basic cloud computing data center demand is increasing. While the Iran crisis impacts these strategic projects, they are largely financed and supported by revenues that originate with oil and gas production and export. The stakes are higher for Saudi Arabia to restore its reputation and investment destination profile. Its national oil company is increasingly more connected to the growing global digital economy and buildout than before.

Given oil demand of roughly 105-6 million barrels per day in 2026 and a peak expected of 112 m/b/d by recent analysis, this bodes well for Aramco. It will be an interesting day if the firm dual lists on a global exchange, which would bring more liquidity (but maybe volatility at times) to the equation. However, the value of liquidity, a rethink of backup infrastructure, and spare capacity shifts could bring that day closer at hand.

-------

More commentary from yesterday: "Oil Crisis Shifts Players..."

data.title: Redwire: Big Opportunities, But Fundamentals Still Lag
data.description:
Redwire Corporation (RDW) is a stock that I have not revisited for some time now. The company continues to be interesting to me for the same reason it originally attracted my attention. The company operates in two of the fastest-growing and in-demand markets: space and drones. The drone industry has exploded in the past year. I have invested in a handful of drone companies over the past year and seen them do well. The space industry is really just getting started as well. There is continued advancement within this market. I think the defense industry and space industry will become much more entwined as time goes on. Space is likely the next frontier for war.

I rated the stock a hold back in August of 2025. The company is down approximately 5% since that time, so it was the right decision to put a hold on the stock. There has been some volatility in that window, with the stock reaching a low of $4.87 late in 2025 and a high of over $14 a share in February. There was an opportunity for traders to profit from the stock.

Despite the company operating in a high-demand market and also a market that I have been looking to invest in, the company has underperformed the market. There were a handful of reasons that I avoided the stock previously: shrinking organic business, profitability issues, poor execution and integration risks, and also dilution. These issues seem to have continued to plague the stock, and I think a large reason the stock has been stuck in neutral.

Financials, Profitability Concerns, Spending Problems

The company reported revenue growth for the 4th quarter and for the year. Revenue for the year reached $335.4 million, up 10.3% versus the prior year. Not a really strong growth rate for the year, but Q4 looked much better, with revenues hitting $108.8 million, good for growth of 56.4% vs. the prior year. The revenue growth was mostly driven by the acquisition of Edge Autonomy and the drone market. Redwire acquired the company in June of 2025, so the last two quarters saw a jump in revenues from the acquisition.

The big concern here that comes up and was a concern I had before is that when you break it down, the business without Edge Autonomy was actually shrinking. That held true through the end of the year. Edge Autonomy contributed $107.1 million post-acquisition. If we back that out of the 2025 revenue figure, we are left with $228.3 million in revenue for the organic business. Revenues in 2024 were $304.1 million. That is a decline of $75 million and down 25% from the prior year. The organic business is not just slowing down; it is falling off a cliff, losing a quarter of its revenues. The company also breaks its business out into two segments: Space and Defense Tech. Edge Autonomy falls within the Defense Tech. The Space segment saw revenues decline by 17.8% from the prior year. If we take out Edge Autonomy, then the Defense Tech business fell by 62.1%. I get that the company shifted focus and pushed more of their effort and investment into Edge after the acquisition, but their first half of the year revenue looked similar. It was more of Edge rescuing the reporting rather than cannibalizing sales.

The profitability of the company also took a hit during the year. Despite revenues growing, gross profit decreased $27.2 million for the year. The company reported a gross margin of 5% for the year, down from an already not good 15% in the prior year. The company is never going to make a profit with that kind of gross margin. The company noted that this was due to a large $58 million EAC adjustment. The company recognizes revenue based on the proportion of total costs incurred to total estimated costs-at-completion (“EAC”). If the project runs over budget, then the company records an EAC Adjustment. This is not an impairment charge or some non-cash event. I think a $58 million EAC adjustment means that the company underestimated their costs and/or underbid a project. That is an operational issue. This is not a one-off expense but rather bad management of their projects, in my view. It is not a one-off issue that should be backed out of the calculations; it is the actual performance of the operations. If they have a poor history of performing, then it is likely to happen again.

Operating income saw the same issues with declines. The company reported an operating loss of $81 million for Q4, which is much larger than the $19 million loss seen last year. The company did have an impairment charge of $34.7 million during the quarter, but even backing that out, we see a loss of $46.9 million, more than doubling from the prior year. The impairment charge does not really explain the loss. For the year the company had an operating loss of $229.7 million compared to $42.2 million in the prior year.

The biggest increase was the increase to SG&A. The company made some acquisitions and added a lot of personnel, but I believe spending needs to be reined in. The company saw its SG&A increase by 159% in Q4 compared to the prior year. SG&A hit $47.8 million in Q4, up from $18.4 million; that is a dollar increase of nearly $30 million. That caused SG&A as a percentage of revenue to increase from 26.5% to 43.9%. For the year, SG&A increased by nearly $100 million. That is a crazy increase in SG&A for the quarter and year. If the company wanted to get SG&A back to 26.5% of revenue, the 2024 figure, it would have to generate revenues of $646.6 million. That is if SG&A sees no increase. Revenue growth is not nearly fast enough for the increases seen in SG&A. The company has a spending problem in my mind that needs to get reined in, or it has no chance to get near a net profit anytime soon.

The cash position is the last item to note. The company sold shares during the year to help fund the Edge acquisition and continued expansion of the business. This left it with a stronger cash position to end the year. The company had $95.2 million in cash, plus its credit line gives the company $130 million in liquidity with which to work. This gives the company some wiggle room, but at the rate it burned through cash in 2025, it likely won't last long. Redwire had free cash flow of negative $200.6 million in 2025. The company needs to get more profitable and quick; otherwise, another dilution event is likely.

Outlook

The company guided for 2026 revenue of $450 million to $500 million. That would be growth of 34% on the low end and 49% on the high end. That would be a good year for the company. This gave investors some hope. I am hesitant about the guidance, though, after last year. To begin 2025, the company guided for revenues of $535-605 million, which it then revised down to $470-530, and then again lowered to $380-445 million. Those are some big downward revisions. I think the estimate is much more reasonable than the original estimate in the prior year. The company has also had a lot of wins lately that should help drive that growth.

The drone business is a catalyst going forward. The market is in hot demand, and the company has positioned itself well with its Edge acquisition. The company announced late last year that it was delivering additional UAS, Penguin model, to the Ukraine Armed Forces. Its technology is being used in current wars and is not just theoretical. It has seen momentum in other parts of Europe as well. It announced a contract win in Croatia and also opened up an office in the UK to help meet the growing demand in the market. The company recently announced that it opened a new facility in Michigan to increase the production of fuel cells for its UAS. I think this shows the growing demand that the company is seeing for its UAS platforms.

The space business has also had some contract wins. Management had previously noted that some contracts were being pushed out and should materialize in 2026. We are starting to see some of those contracts being awarded. The biggest win was a $44 million contract from DARPA. It is related to very low earth orbit (“VLEO”), which is becoming a growing market for the company, as it is the prime on a contract with the European Space Agency for the same technology. The most recent news was related to Redwire’s involvement in the Artemis II mission. The company is starting to see some wins in the space business and hoping that translates to growth, or at least stops the decline for the business.

These wins are showing up in the backlog for the company. This was an area of concern for me previously, as the backlog was showing limited growth. The company ended the year with a backlog of $411.2 million. This is less than the total year revenue estimate, but the company was awarded $165.9 million in contracts during Q4. Most of that came within the space business as well, with awards of $110.9 million. That pace is higher than the average quarterly revenues are expected to be during 2026. So if the company can keep that pace, they will continue to grow that backlog. That should provide a lot of assurances for investors.

Other Concerns

Dilution has been and continues to be very large. The company ended the year with 191.9 million shares outstanding, up from 67 million in the prior year. An increase of 186%. A large part of this, to the tune of 94 million shares, was related to the Edge acquisition. Even taking those shares out of the equation, the company share count increased by 45.7% during the year. So while the stock price was stuck in the mud, the market cap actually saw a pretty good increase. All of the increase went into additional shares rather than increasing the price of the already outstanding shares. I think dilution is likely to happen again. The company is burning through a lot of cash. While it has $130 million in liquidity available, I do not see that as sufficient to continue to fund the business.

SBC was another concern for me. This is contributing to dilution as well. The company had SBC of $59 million in 2025, up from $11.3 million in 2024. That is nearly 18% of revenues. The company already has dilution concerns as it keeps raising money due to losses. Even without those raises, shareholders are being diluted with SBC that high. The argument can be made that the SBC is up a lot due to the acquisition of Edge Autonomy and management awards, but the figure for the year was already high without any of that. In Q4 the company had $11.4 million in SBC; at an annual rate, that would be approximately $45 million. The company had SBC of $59 million for the year. So Edge Autonomy does not explain the high figure for the rest of the year.

Valuation

Redwire has a market cap of $1.77 billion. It is hard to value on a profit multiple since the company is operating at a loss. We can look at a PS ratio. The company has a P/S ratio of 4. That is not bad when you consider the revenue growth estimate for 2026 is over 40%. That would bring the PS ratio down to 3.76 for 2026, which is not a high premium for a company with strong growth. The real question for me is whether or not the company will be able to hit those estimates, which it has had a hard time doing in the past. Also, will the growth continue in 2027?

The real concern about the value is around profitability. The company has really large losses. Management guided for EBITDA break-even in 2025 to start the year but did not come close to achieving that goal. It has done the same for 2026, stating that it will reach break-even EBITDA by the end of the year. I do not see that happening based upon the current financial situation. With 8 analysts covering the stock, the high estimate for EPS in 2027 is $-0.22. Pretty high confidence that the company will be at least 2028 before turning a profit.

The value for the company lies in the future potential and markets in which the company operates. The space and drone industries are both strong, growing markets. Investors are hoping that Redwire can carve out a piece of that market.

Conclusion

I really like where Redwire is operating. Space and drones are as hot as you get in the market, other than AI. I have invested in both industries and am intrigued by companies that operate in these markets. I had some concerns about Redwire previously, and it caused me to avoid the stock. Many of those concerns persist, and some even got worse in my view.

The company has struggled to execute and run efficiently. It took steps back in terms of profitability. Gross margin decreased, and expenses increased at a much higher rate than revenues. I think spending within the company has been too high, and it needs to reduce expenses. The other option is for the company to grow revenues at a high clip for multiple years to make up for the spend. It would take multiple years of high revenue growth and no increases in spending to make up for the added expenses. The company has also seen large dilution through capital raises and SBC. I think further dilution is likely for the company. SBC has also been increasing each quarter.

The company is in a good niche. It also has some good momentum entering the year. Backlog showed some real strength, and it makes it much more likely that the company will achieve year-end revenue estimates. Even with that momentum, I do not want to own the stock here. There are too many negatives and concerns for me at the moment. I will continue to monitor the stock but will stay on the sidelines here.

data.title: Telephone And Data Systems: Preferreds Remain The Best Income Play Post Deleveraging
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, I saw Telephone and Data Systems, Inc. (TDS) Preferred Shares (TDS.PR.U) as the most attractive way for income investors to play the TDS business transformation story, as the company made an important strategic pivot to monetize its mobile assets, namely through the sale of US Cellular and spectrum sales to other operators.

Given that its common stock is currently offering a dividend yield of about 0.35%, while the Preferred UU shares yield close to 8%, this seems to continue to be clearly attractive to income-oriented investors despite the strong price appreciation of the UU Preferred Shares over the past couple of years.

Indeed, the UU Preferred Shares continue to trade at a discount to its liquidation value, and, contrary to what I was expecting some months ago, the company has decided to skip the call recently and has used cash proceeds from asset sales toward debt reduction instead and performing share buybacks.

Indeed, since my last article on TDS, the company has closed the sale of US Cellular and some spectrum assets to T-Mobile US (TMUS) for about $4.3 billion, of which some $2.6 billion was cash and $1.7 billion was debt assumed by TMUS. This was a very important milestone for the company, allowing it to monetize its most important asset at the time, leading to much lower balance sheet leverage following the sale.

Furthermore, its new unit called Array Digital Infrastructure, which holds the spectrum and tower assets, closed another spectrum sale last January with AT&T (T) for more than $1 billion, raising further cash and allowing TDS to pay bank debt and invest in its fiber rollout in the wireline segment. Also, investors should note that Array still has two spectrum sales pending with Verizon (VZ) and TMUS, which should raise about $1 billion on a combined basis. This is another important step to monetize assets that weren’t included in the US Cellular deal, while further sales are likely in the near future as the company wants to streamline its operations and monetize mobile assets.

Following these transactions and significant cash raised, TDS has reduced its net debt levels from about $2.4 billion at the end of 2024 to some $823 million by year-end 2025. This is a very significant reduction and shows that TDS’ balance sheet is now in a much stronger position following the asset sales, being a very positive development for the Preferred shares and largely explaining why the UU Preferred series share price has recovered substantially from its bottom levels around $10 per share back in 2023.

Moreover, the spectrum sale to AT&T was closed in January, and TDS ’used the proceeds to additionally reduce debt by $150 million, which means TDS’ net debt should be below $600 million at the end of Q1 2026. As the company’s guidance is to have an adjusted EBITDA between $310 million and $350 million in 2026, this means its net debt-to-EBITDA ratio should be between 1.7 and 19.x at year-end 2026.

This is a very acceptable ratio within the telecoms sector, considering that due to the capital-intensive business model of telecom companies, leverage ratios are usually relatively high. Most of its peers usually target a leverage ratio around 2.5x, given that this is a level that credit rating agencies usually require to maintain investment-grade credit ratings across the sector.

This means TDS is now in a strong balance sheet position following the disposal of the mobile assets, not having much need for further balance sheet deleveraging ahead. Therefore, I think the company is likely to use most of its free cash flow to finance business growth and return capital to shareholders, namely by investing in the fiber rollout in the wireline segment and performing share buybacks.

Investors should note that TDS operations are now focused on the wireline segment and the towers business, while the remaining mobile spectrum is expected to be fully divested in the near term.

Considering this business profile, TDS now has a stable operation with some growth prospects, especially related to the fiber expansion strategy, in which TDS wants to cover more existing customers by its fiber network and expand to new locations over the coming years. This has been its major growth engine in the wireline segment and is expected to continue to be in the near future, being an important tailwind for revenue growth over the next few years. As shown in the next graph, the total number of addresses covered by the company has increased significantly in recent years, a trend that is expected to be maintained ahead.

These strong investments in fiber network expansion mean TDS is expected to maintain relatively high levels of capital expenditure over the next few years. Its guidance is to have capex between $550 million and $600 million in 2026, compared to $406 million in the previous year, showing that TDS is pushing for network expansion as an important growth driver.

Despite that, its guidance is to report annual revenues between $1.015 billion and $1.055 billion in the wireline segment during 2026, practically flat compared to the previous year at the middle of its guidance range. This is explained by some weakness in the commercial and wholesale segments that should offset to a large extent revenue growth in the residential segment.

In the Towers segment, the company owned about 4,450 towers at the end of 2025, collecting rental revenue from the three major telecom operators in the U.S., even though TMUS is naturally the largest customer following the sale of US Cellular. As part of the sale, TDS made a master license agreement for about 2,000 towers for 15 years with TMUS, providing a long-term revenue stream for the Array segment.

Given that TMUS generates more than 60% of Array’s revenues, there is clearly significant customer concentration, but the risk for Array is limited due to the long-term nature of rental contracts leading to a stable and predictable revenue and earnings stream over the long term.

For 2026, TDS guidance is to generate revenues between $200 million and $215 million in the Array unit, with adjusted EBITDA in a similar amount due to earnings from unconsolidated entities.

Overall, its total revenues should be around $1.27 billion in 2026, up by about 3% YoY, and its EBITDA is expected to be around $546 million, representing an increase of 4% from the previous year. This shows that TDS’ growth prospects are somewhat limited, being largely driven by its investments in fiber network expansion. In the near term, I think an important catalyst to unlock value can be the sale of the remaining spectrum, which is valued at about $1.6 billion in the company’s books, which will further strengthen the company’s balance sheet and financial flexibility. Most likely, TDS will use proceeds from spectrum sales to reduce debt, invest in fiber, and return capital to shareholders.

However, dividends related to its common stock have been stable at $0.04 per share on a quarterly basis, and TDS is likely to maintain its strategy of performing share buybacks instead of growing its dividend or distributing special dividends to shareholders, making its Preferred shares the most attractive plays for income investors. Both its UU and VV series offer yields close to 8%, which are now safer following the balance sheet overhaul of the past couple of years, making them quite interesting for income-oriented investors.

Conclusion

Telephone and Data Systems has deleveraged quite significantly its balance sheet over the past couple of years by monetizing its mobile assets both through the sale of its stake in US Cellular and spectrum sales. While this process is largely completed, the company still has some spectrum assets that it wants to sell in the near future, being a good setback for further financial flexibility ahead. Given this backdrop, its Preferred shares are the best way to play this theme, given that its common stock offers a very low dividend yield.

data.title: Amazon: Stronger Than Ever With Globalstar Deal
data.description:
Shares of Amazon (AMZN) made a convincing move higher, trading within the immediate vicinity of all-time highs. Investors apparently like the announced deal for Globalstar, which marks a big step in expanding the Amazon Leo satellite network.

I like this move (similar to other deals we cover at Value In Corporate Events), with Amazon continuing to have ever larger ambitions and a successful track record in pursuing them. Strong growth and strong profits provide more than ample firepower to aggressively and rightfully continue to invest in its future.

Moving Into Space

Amazon has reached a deal to acquire Globalstar, allowing its LEO service to add direct-to-direct services to lower Earth orbit satellite networks to extend cellular networks beyond terrestrial networks. This service will also power Apple's Watch, including Emergency SOS by satellite.

Such efforts are part of Amazon's space-based connectivity goals to deliver both reliable and high-speed connectivity to customers, wherever they are in the world.

These solutions allow for acceleration of innovation, expansion of connectivity options, greater resilience in public and private sectors, and closing the digital divide. Investors in Globalstar can elect to receive $90 in cash or 0.3210 shares of Amazon for every share in Globalstar they own, with the total cash consideration capped at 40%. Frankly, the share component surprises me somewhat, as a 0.3210 share of Amazon only represents a value of just over $80 for Globalstar's stock, based on Amazon's shares trading around the $250 mark here.

Investors in Globalstar, combined representing 58% of the voting power, already voted in favor of the deal, which involves a potential $110 million downward adjustment if milestones are not achieved. Operating with a flattish net debt load, the 127 million shares of Globalstar are valued at $11.4 billion at $90 per share. With a 40% cash component, that implies a roughly $4.5 billion cash payment and nearly $7 billion worth of stock to be issued, or just a bit less.

A Bolt-On Deal

It seems strange to say that a >$10 billion deal for Globalstar is really just a bolt-on deal for Amazon, yet a highly strategic one, as is clear from the documentation on the deal.

The cash component will be no issue at all, with Amazon ending 2025 with a gross cash position of around $100 billion, with net cash holdings of around $47 billion. With a diluted share count of about 10.9 billion shares, the company commands just over a $2.7 trillion market valuation, and its enterprise valuation comes in just below that number.

This is applied to a company growing sales by about 12% to $717 billion as operating profits of nearly $80 billion further surpassed the 10% mark, this for a long time being a highly breakeven business. This is driven by growth in AWS, whose revenues rose by 20% to nearly $129 billion, with operating margins reported in the mid-thirties, as a 24% growth number for the final quarter is reassuring. North American sales rose by 10% to $426 billion, with operating margins seen near 7% of sales. International sales grew a bit faster at around 13% to $162 billion, yet margins are stuck just below 3% here.

All in all, this business is valued at less than 4 times sales, as GAAP earnings of $77 billion came in just north of $7 per share. This number benefited from a $17 billion non-operating income benefit, as otherwise earnings come in at a lower level, shy of $6 per share, making the shares trade around 45 times trailing earnings.

The company invested about $128 billion in 2025, that number essentially twice the $65 billion depreciation and amortization expense, marking large net capital investments, those equal to about reported earnings. However, this is not seen ending soon, with capital spending pegged at $200 billion for 2026. Note that capital investments will not necessarily increase by the same degree as depreciation expenses trend near $80 billion based on the fourth quarter depreciation expense, increasing rapidly as well.

What Now?

In the grand scheme of things, the Globalstar deal is very tiny, equivalent to about 0.3-0.4% of the enterprise valuation, making a >$10 billion deal really a bolt-on deal. More important is willingness to make deals to actively invest, but moreover to expand activities into space and communications as well, with the business actively involved in space and AI as well.

Since last covering shares in early spring of 2024, shares have risen some 40%, those returns largely in line with the markets, after a spectacular 20% run higher recently. This includes a solid 4% move higher in response to the Globalstar deal, clearly indicating a sign of confidence, as over a hundred billion in market value has been added in response to essentially a $10 billion deal.

The reality is that between overall growth and, moreover, scale growth in retail operations and higher growth in AWS, which carries above-average margins, company-wide operating margins are increasing. This will help to drive earnings growth even as the first quarter guidance, which called for 13% sales growth, suggests just a modest increase in operating profit in dollar terms.

Trading at a high thirty times forward earnings multiple, valuations are demanding.
