Of course, this includes a huge retail business, but then again, Walmart (WMT) trades at similar or even higher multiples, while Amazon has a huge AWS business and a great track record of growing into adjacent and profitable segments.

Two years ago I could easily see Amazon doing a trillion in sales in 2030, as that number now seems conservative, requiring just 40% revenue growth until the end of the decade. Moreover, I pegged operating margins at 10-12% at that point, as these already came in at 11% in 2025, making the company firmly on track to exceed the guidance.

This makes me happy to hang onto a modest position, one that I have held for a long time. Amazon rightfully deserves a premium, as the latest dealmaking and focus only add to its track record, creating a competitor for Starlink as well.

Given all these trends, I'm perfectly happy to hold onto a position but in no rush to chase the shares here after momentum lately. Momentum has been too strong to go chase the shares here, but the long-term investment thesis seems more than fully intact, perhaps stronger than ever before.

data.title: BUCK Offers A 7.55% Yield, But Don't Ignore The Competition
data.description:
BUCK Strategy

Simplify Treasury Option Income ETF (BUCK) is an actively managed bond and options income ETF launched on 10/27/2022. BUCK has a 12-month trailing yield of 7.55% and an expense ratio of 0.35%. Distributions are paid monthly.

As described by Symplify ETFs, the fund primarily invests in U.S. Treasuries and ETFs holding U.S. Treasuries while maintaining an aggregate duration of one year or less. Additionally, the fund executes a call spread strategy and a put spread strategy to enhance income. A call spread consists of selling an out-of-the-money (above current market price) call option and purchasing a further out-of-the-money call option. A put spread consists of selling an out-of-the-money (below current market price) put option and purchasing a further out-of-the-money put option. The fund's managers expect the sold options to expire worthless and generate income from premium. Options are usually sold with less than one month to expiration. The options bought by the fund reduce income but limit losses in case the underlying asset’s price goes against the strategy. Short-term bond instruments and cash equivalents are used as collateral for the options strategies.

Portfolio

Virtually all of the net asset value (99.9%) is invested in Simplify Government Money Market ETF (SBIL). SBIL has a distribution rate of 3.24% and a trailing 12-month yield of 2.68% and is primarily invested in short-term U.S. government securities and repurchase agreements. It is at the same time BUCK’s core income instrument and collateral for the options strategies.

Additionally, the fund holds six positions in U.S. bond future options expiring in May and June.

BUCK’s share price is almost insensitive to rate variations: the effective duration is close to zero (0.04 year).

Performance

BUCK has outperformed the Treasury bill benchmark State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) by 1.9% in total return since inception (about 60 bps annualized). It shows a higher risk based on drawdowns (maximum -5.4%) and volatility.

In the same time, the share price has lost about 6%.

Distributions have been quite variable, as plotted on the next chart. The latest paid value ($0.14 on 3/31/2026) is equal to the historical average.

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of BUCK and five options income ETFs based on a bond portfolio:

  • iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (TLTW).
  • iShares Investment Grade Corporate Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (LQDW).
  • iShares High Yield Corporate Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (HYGW).
  • NEOS Enhanced Income 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (CSHI).
  • NEOS Enhanced Income Aggregate Bond ETF (BNDI).

This list is not intended to be exhaustive. In particular, it excludes ETFs with less than 3 years of track record, such as NEOS Enhanced Income 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLTI), NEOS Enhanced Income Credit Select ETF (HYBI), and Global X Treasury Bond Enhanced Income ETF (TLTX).

Takeaway

Simplify Treasury Option Income ETF has a 7.55% yield from options spreads and a portfolio of short-term treasuries and cash equivalents. BUCK has outperformed a Treasury bill benchmark by about 60 bps annualized since inception, with significantly higher risk (5.4% drawdown) and moderate capital erosion. Among ETFs blending bonds and options income, CSHI is a more compelling alternative despite a lower yield, with a slightly higher total return, stable asset value, and muted volatility.
